retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Spleen -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The spleen contains two major units: white pulp and red pulp. [2] The white pulp is composed of lymphatic tissue surrounding a central arteriole and contains mainly white blood cells that are involved in the initiation of the adaptive immune response. The innermost area of the white pulp, the germinal center, contains B-cells while the surrounding marginal zone contains T-cells. [3] The marginal zone is surrounded by a periarteriolar lymphoid sheath (PALS), which also contains T-cells. White pulp throughout the spleen is surrounded by red pulp. The red pulp is composed of splenic cords (Cords of Billroth) and a large volume of venous sinuses, which gives the structure its characteristic red appearance under a microscopic. The splenic cords provide the organ structure through reticulin and fibrils. The cords also contain a reservoir of monocytes to aid in wound healing. Splenic cords lead to splenic sinuses where macrophages respond to antigens and filter abnormal or aging erythrocytes out of blood flow. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The pulp mass is a highly vascularized and innervated mass of connective tissue that resides within a spaced called the pulp chamber. Various cell types characterize this tissue, including fibroblasts, odontoblasts, histiocytes, macrophages, mast cells, and plasma cells. It also contains an extracellular matrix composed of collagenous fibers and ground substance. Given that the pulp forms the layer of dentin evenly in every direction, it effectively shapes itself into miniaturization of the tooth and reflects the external form of the enamel. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: There are two main regions of pulpal architecture: the central and the peripheral. On the periphery of the pulp, the area adjacent to the calcified dentin exists a series of different structural layers. At the interface of the dentin-pulp complex exists a layer of columnar odontoblast cells, whose primary function is the development of dentin. Here, many dentinal tubules, channels created by the odontoblasts, extend through the dentin from the pulp to the enamel border. [3] Each one of these tubules contains an odontoblast process, a cellular extension of an odontoblast used to form the dentin, as well as dentinal fluid. Like enamel, dentin is avascular, and these tubules are crucial for providing an entryway for nutrients in the interstitial fluid that originated from capillaries in the pulp. Beneath the odontoblastic layer is the cell-free zone, or zone of Weil, an area rich in both capillaries and nerve networks. The peripheral compartment closest to the central zone is the cell-rich zone, a layer rich in fibroblasts and undifferentiated mesenchymal cell that functions in supporting the population of odontoblasts by proliferation and differentiation. This cell layer can also differentiate into fibroblasts and macrophages. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The blood supply to the pulp arises from arterioles that make their way from the periodontium into the pulp via the apical foramen and through the radicular pulp. Each pulp chamber contains 1 to 2 arterioles and one large venule that branches into an extensive capillary network in the periphery of the crown. The network generally occupies the area under the odontoblast layer, sometimes even reaches into it. Despite the tissue pressure in the pulp being higher than other bodily tissues, the blood flow within the capillaries is comparatively higher. It causes a bulk flow of fluid across the capillaries into the tissue. As a result, fluid exits through lymphatic vessels at an equal rate to maintain a constant tissue fluid volume. Moreover, the high tissue pressure of the pulp facilitates outflow through the dentinal tubules, which functions to wash away toxins and bacteria. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe inflammatory reactions can lead to pulpal cell injury, potentially compromising pulpal vitality and survivability. Although the infection activates an immune response, immune cells and molecules are unable to effectively penetrate dentin to remove pathogens due to the narrow apical foramen of the tooth and the hard enamel surrounding the internal structures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The central pulp zone's perimeter is outlined by the edge of the cell-rich layer. This body of tissue is the main support system for the peripheral region and contains large vessels and nerves that extend out into the periphery. Similar to the cell-rich zones, it also contains many fibroblasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to a decrease in lymphoid follicles, there may be an increase in the red pulp, which is responsible for filtering and storing red blood cells. Histopathology can provide essential insights into the underlying pathology of functional asplenism. In addition, the characteristic decrease in white pulp and increase in red pulp can help diagnose and manage various conditions associated with functional asplenism, including sickle cell disease, thalassemia, and autoimmune disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Accessory Spleen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The normal spleen is a versatile organ responsible for a variety of functions including filtering blood and producing immunity. Each function is carried out by specialized tissues in the spleen called red pulp and white pulp. Red pulp is made of connective tissue helping to form cords and sinuses containing white blood cells (WBCs). The sinuses create empty spaces which allow blood to flow through and be exposed to WBCs. This filtration helps to remove old and damaged red blood cells and destroy foreign microbes such as viruses, bacteria, and fungi. The white pulp, on the other hand, is more intricately divided than the red pulp. The white pulp consists of three areas including the periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths, lymph follicles, and marginal zone. The periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths are filled with T lymphocytes and surround the arteriole blood supply. The lymph follicles, on the other hand, are filled with B lymphocytes and are located between the periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths and the marginal zone. The marginal zone is filled with antigen-presenting cells. The white pulp as a whole is responsible for the production and maturation of WBCs. [3] The accessory spleen is morphologically and physiologically similar to a normal spleen and is therefore composed of a comparable subset of structures which carry out similar functions.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The physiology of the pulp can change over time due to the aging process, resulting in distinct phenotypic differences. Compromised circulation and innervation occurs due to increased deposition of secondary dentin at the apical root, constricting the apical foramen. Blood vessels, lymphatic vessels, and nerves entering aged pulp demonstrate arteriosclerotic changes, degenerative changes, and progressive mineralization of the nerve sheath, respectively. The nerve death that occurs in aged pulp explains why aged teeth are frequently painless. Remaining odontoblasts become smaller and have a flattened morphology. Despite there being a profound reduction in cell density (about half from 20 to 70 years old), another characteristic of aged pulp is an increase in fibrosis and collagenous fibers. Due to the reduction in fibroblasts, this apparent increase is not due to increased production of collagenous fibers, but rather reflects the sheaths of connective tissue that remain after the death of nerves and blood vessels to become part of the fibrous matrix. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Organ Systems Involved
retrieved_docs.content: The spleen has a function similar to lymph nodes. T cells and B cells are in the white pulp. Germinal centers within the white pulp contain many memory cells, follicular dendritic cells, and macrophages.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: It is important for the dental pulp to sufficiently clear tissue fluid and cell components within its microcirculation through the presence of an effective lymphatic drainage system, although the exact functioning remains unclear. It has been postulated that lymph is collected in interstitial tissue clefts within the coronal pulp and is transported to lymphatic capillaries via the apical pulp. [22] The capillary walls are composed of a single layer of endothelial cells, which allows for the absorption of lymph from tissue fluid. Once transported to lymph nodes, the lymph is filtered and exposed to lymphocytes. All teeth drain to the submandibular lymph nodes, except the mandibular incisors, which drain the submental lymph nodes. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Recent studies show that the possibility to regenerate the dental pulp exists, through transplantation of dental pulp stem cells with pulpitis, on a human model.

retrieved_docs.title: Asplenia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histologically, atrophy of the spleen is evident by degeneration of white pulp (ie, decreased periarteriolar lymphatic sheaths, or PALS, decreased follicles, germinal centers, and marginal zones). Decreased amounts of hematopoietic components characterize red pulp atrophy. Cytologically, the presence of Howell-Jolly bodies and pitted red blood cells visible upon review of a peripheral blood smear may suggest asplenia or reduced function of the spleen. Other non-specific cytologic findings include absolute monocytosis, lymphocytosis, and/or thrombocytosis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Spleen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The spleen has several functions, including the filtering of blood, removing microbes and inadequate red blood cells (RBCs), producing white blood cells (WBCs), and antibody synthesis. It is important to note, that while the spleen does have a wide range of functions, it is not a vital organ. Individuals can survive without a spleen as other organs of the body, such as the liver, can adapt in its absence to serve just about the same functions. The spleen consists of 2 different tissue types, termed white pulp and red pulp, with each tissue type serving unique functions. White pulp is composed of periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths (PALS) and lymphatic nodules. The white pulp tissue is involved with the production and maturity of WBCs, particularly lymphocytes (types B and T) and thereby the production of antibodies. The red pulp is composed of splenic sinusoids (wide blood vessels) and cords/threads of connective tissue. The red pulp tissue is involved more so with the filtering aspect of the blood. The red pulp removes old, damaged, and/or useless red blood cells. Contained within the red pulp are also WBCs, particularly phagocytes (macrophages in particular) which destroy microorganisms such as viruses, bacteria, and fungi. The red pulp also acts as a storage area for WBCs and platelets, which are typically released to injury sites to aid in healing and inflammation regulation or to assist in blood loss compensation. The white and red pulp regions are separated by a border known as the marginal zone which functions as a filter, filtering pathogens out of the blood and into the white pulp. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The defensive role of the pulp occurs through the development of new dentin, which can provide a barrier between irritants and slow the rate of carious decay. This process is mediated by the pulp, which spurs odontoblasts into action or initiates the production of new ones to form this needed hard tissue. The type and amount of dentin produced depend on numerous factors, including the source of damage (pathogen, thermal, chemical), the depth of damage, its severity, and the amount of surface area involved. The fact odontoblasts underline the significance of the pulp in dentin formation do not participate in cell division. Consequently, the formation of additional dentin relies on the pool of undifferentiated mesenchymal populations in the cell-rich zone to replace them. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Hairy Cell Leukemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: HCL variant: Typically has prominent nucleoli and less marrow infiltration. It is often associated with extreme leukocytosis, often without the neutropenia, monocytopenia, anemia, and thrombocytopenia seen in HCL. Splenic diffuse red pulp small B cell lymphoma: Typically does not express annexin A1, CD25, CD103, CD123, and CD11c. Splenic marginal zone lymphoma: Typically does not express CD103, CD11c, and CD25. Other unclassifiable splenic lymphomas Mantle cell lymphoma: Expresses CD5 and has strong expression of cyclin D1; it does not express CD25, CD103, or annexin A1. It also does not demonstrate hairy cytoplasm in lymphocytes. Chronic lymphocytic leukemia: Expresses CD5 and lacks expression of CD103. It involves both red pulp and white pulp of the spleen while HCV predominantly involves red pulp. Prolymphocytic leukemia: Marked the elevation of the white blood cell count, with the characteristic morphology of prolymphocytes and lack of hairy cytoplasmic projections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1039
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. As malignant tumors arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head and the thymus—each represents an uncontrolled growth of neoplastic cells that characterizes a serious pathological condition. Recognizing these neoplasms as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations emphasizes their role as aggressive cancerous processes that require prompt diagnostic evaluation and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are all instances of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled malignant cell growth. Each condition represents a distinct organ‑specific cancer that arises as a primary tumor (thyroid, pancreatic head, thymus) and, in the case of the thyroid cancer, can spread to regional lymph nodes in the neck. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their shared nature as serious pathological processes that require comprehensive oncologic assessment, staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 873
retrieved_docs.content: Neoplasm of lymph node encompasses the specific entity ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, which represents a lymph‑node tumor. This tumor is anatomically linked to the Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System, reflecting the system’s role as the primary site associated with the development of such lymph‑node neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
retrieved_docs.content: Amphotericin B is identified as a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat diseases of white blood cells, indicating its potential role in managing conditions that affect the immune cell population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2407
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with both السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer) and سرطانة العظم (bone cancer), indicating the presence of malignant disease in these organs. In contrast, Tumor cells, benign are explicitly noted as not being findings of either السرطان البنكرياسي or سرطانة العظم, reflecting that benign tumor cells do not signify these malignant conditions. Conversely, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as abnormal cells of disease for both السرطان البنكرياسي and سرطانة العظم, underscoring their role as the pathological cellular component characteristic of these cancers. This distinction between malignant infiltrates, benign tumor cells, and malignant tumor cells is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management in cases of pancreatic and bone malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding associated with السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue specimens indicates that cancerous cells have invaded pancreatic tissue, thereby supporting the diagnosis of السرطان البنكرياسي. Identification of this infiltrate through histopathological examination informs clinical staging, guides therapeutic decision‑making, and is essential for optimal patient management in cases of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: connect broader cancer classifications, specific primary malignancies (including thymic, pancreatic, nasal‑bone, and thyroid‑derived lesions), and specialized subsets such as Hodgkin’s disease and malignant blood‑tumor groups. Understanding this interconnected hierarchy supports accurate classification, diagnosis, and management of hematopoietic, immune‑system, and solid‑organ cancers involving lymph‑node involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 585
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings encompass a broad spectrum of atypical laboratory results, and within this category a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) is identified as a specific child finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.
